questions.prompt: What does a country's Gross Domestic Product (GDP) measure?

options.text: The value of all goods and services produced in the country over one year
The total amount of household savings
The quantity of money in circulation
The State's debt owed to foreign creditors

questions.explanation: GDP adds up the value of all the goods and services a country produces over one year: it is the main indicator of its economic activity. Its rise from one year to the next is called growth. Don't confuse it with savings or debt, which measure something else entirely.

questions.prompt: According to the law of supply and demand, what generally happens when demand for a good rises sharply while supply stays steady?

options.text: The price of the good tends to fall
The price of the good tends to rise
The price stays exactly the same
The good disappears from the market for good

questions.explanation: When demand outstrips supply, the good becomes relatively scarce and its price rises toward the equilibrium price. The price acts as a signal of scarcity. It does not stay frozen: it is precisely its adjustment that balances the quantities supplied and demanded.

options.text: A fall in a country's population
The creation of a new currency
A general and lasting rise in prices
An increase in exports

questions.explanation: Inflation is the general and lasting rise in the price level: your money loses purchasing power. Its opposite, deflation, is a general fall in prices. Inflation of 5% means a basket that cost 100 now costs 105.

questions.prompt: Which branch of economics studies the behaviour of an individual agent such as a household or a firm?

options.text: Macroeconomics
Microeconomics
Geopolitics
National accounting

questions.explanation: Microeconomics studies the choices of individual agents (a household, a firm), while macroeconomics observes a country's economy as a whole (unemployment, growth, inflation). The prefix "micro" (small) helps tell them apart.

questions.prompt: Which of these is NOT a function of money?

options.text: Serving as a medium of exchange to pay
Serving as a unit of account to measure value
Serving as a store of value to save
Single-handedly setting the country's unemployment rate

questions.explanation: Money fulfils three functions: medium of exchange, unit of account, and store of value. The unemployment rate depends on the labour market and the economic climate, not on money alone. Without money, we would have to go back to barter, which is far less practical.
